summary: I was born in Prague, which is now part of the Czech Republic. It was there in the 1930s and during World War II that my early life was shaped. My grandfather was a Unitarian minister in Prague and suffered greatly under the Gestapo during the German occupation. Those years in Prague left a deep impression on me and influenced my decision to move to the United States after the war.

Prague, where I was born, is now in the Czech Republic. Back in the 1930s and 1940s, it was Czechoslovakia, but after the war and the communist takeover in 1948, my family and I were separated. I stayed in the United States while my family returned to what became the communist-controlled Czech Republic. It was a difficult time politically and personally.

My mother was very fond of the United States and spoke English without an accent, which helped me become bilingual. After World War II, in 1946, my whole family came to the United States, but they eventually returned to Europe. I stayed here from age fourteen, went to high school in Tarrytown, New York, and later attended the University of Wisconsin–Madison. I also served in the United States Army during the Korean War, which was a defining experience for me.

I mention Henry Kissinger when talking about accents because, like me, he has a noticeable one. We both come from European backgrounds and ended up in the United States. I joke that my accent might not be as bad as his, but it’s a reminder of our shared immigrant experiences. Kissinger is a well-known figure who also has roots in Central Europe.

World War II was a terrifying time for me growing up in Prague under German occupation. We had to listen to shortwave radios secretly to hear news from the BBC, which was punishable by death. My grandfather was arrested by the Gestapo and died in Dachau concentration camp, and my aunt was sent to a forced labor camp. Those years aged me beyond my years and left lasting scars.

Just recently, I ran into a man in Las Vegas who was about my age, and we joked about being 'somewhere between forty and death.' It was a lighthearted moment that reminded me how far I’ve come since those early days in Europe. Las Vegas was just a brief stop in my life’s journey, but it sparked a reflection on age and experience.

My parents both attended the University of California, and my mother even had a scholarship there. They met and married while at that university, and I always thought I should have been born in California. I applied there first after high school, but I wasn’t accepted, so I ended up going to the University of Wisconsin instead. It was a bit of a missed opportunity, but it shaped my path nonetheless.

After high school in Tarrytown, New York, I applied to the University of Wisconsin–Madison and was accepted. I chose it partly because a friend from high school was considering Wisconsin, and it seemed as good as Minnesota. I started there just as the Korean War began, which soon changed my plans. Wisconsin was where I began my American college experience.

I went to high school in Tarrytown, New York after arriving in the United States at age fourteen. It was there that I adjusted to American life and made friends who influenced my college choices. One of my high school classmates was from Illinois and helped me consider the University of Wisconsin. Tarrytown was my first real American home after leaving Europe.

During the German occupation of Prague, the Gestapo was a constant threat to my family. They suspected my grandfather, a Unitarian minister, because he listened to the BBC and spoke hopeful words about the future. One morning, they raided his home and arrested him, leading to his death in Dachau. The Gestapo also took my aunt to a forced labor camp, making those years very dangerous for us.

Listening to the BBC on shortwave radio was illegal and punishable by death during the German occupation of Prague. My family had a shortwave radio and a card to prove we were allowed to listen, but it was still very risky. My grandfather was caught listening to the BBC early one morning, which led to his arrest by the Gestapo. The BBC was a vital source of news and hope for us during those dark times.

My grandfather was taken by the Gestapo and sent to Dachau concentration camp during World War II. Unfortunately, he did not survive the ordeal there. His death was a tragic loss for our family and a harsh reminder of the brutality of the Nazi regime. Dachau represents one of the darkest chapters of my early life in Prague.

I was drafted into the United States Army shortly after starting college during the Korean War. Even though I wasn’t a citizen yet, I stepped forward willingly, knowing it would help my path to citizenship. I trained at Camp Chaffee, Arkansas, and eventually was sent to Korea with the 25th Infantry Division. My time in the Army was intense but rewarding, and I rose to Sergeant First Class within a year.

My basic training took place at Camp Chaffee, Arkansas, where I learned artillery skills with the 105 millimeter howitzer. It was there that I also took language tests and leadership courses, hoping to use my language skills in the Army. Camp Chaffee was the starting point of my military service before being sent overseas to Japan and then Korea.

The Korean War began just as I started college, and it quickly became a reality when I was drafted into the Army. I was scared to go, especially since I knew little about Korea and had hoped to be stationed in Europe. After training in the U.S. and Japan, I was sent to Korea with the 25th Infantry Division, where I served for about a year. The war was a challenging and defining experience for me.

After basic training in the United States, I was sent to Japan, where we stayed at an old Japanese Army base from World War II. There, Japanese personnel helped us prepare and clean M1 Garand rifles, showing great expertise. Japan was a staging area before we were deployed to Korea, and it was interesting to see the remnants of the war there firsthand.

While in Japan preparing for Korea, I saw Japanese soldiers dismantle and clean M1 Garand rifles with impressive skill. They knew more about those rifles than I did after basic training. We tested the rifles at a range before moving on to Korea. The M1 Garand was a key weapon during my military service.

When I arrived in Korea, I was assigned as a replacement to the 25th Infantry Division. Despite being a private, I was welcomed warmly by the officers and soldiers, which helped me adjust quickly. I started at the Punch Bowl area in North Korea and served with them for almost a year, eventually becoming Sergeant First Class and in charge of all six units in our battery.

After leaving Japan, we traveled by boat to Inchon, Korea, where we disembarked and then took a train and jeep to reach our units. Inchon was the entry point for many soldiers like me heading into the Korean War. From there, I was sent to join the 25th Infantry Division at the front lines.

I first joined my unit at the Punch Bowl area in North Korea, which was a significant battle zone during the Korean War. It was a tense place to start, and we were often under shelling and gunfire. Serving there was a tough introduction to combat, but it was where I began my year-long service with the 25th Infantry Division.
